title_tesim: Cast members and extras dancing in the county ball scene from Hatter's Castle
caption_tesim: Robert Newton, James Mason, Deborah Kerr, Emlyn Williams inHatter's Castle.A Paramount Picture
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: From the AFI listing: "Hatter's Castle" was produced by Paramount's British company in 1941, but was not released in the United States until 1948. According to the Los Angeles Times, the picture was held back until its stars were better known to American audiences.
The British film, "Hatter's Castle," released in Great Britain on November 12, 1941, was released in the U.S.A. as "A. J. Cronin's Hatter's Castle," on June 18, 1948.
From the AFI listing: Based on the novel "Hatter's Castle," by A. J. Cronin (Boston, 1931)

Producer, Isadore Goldsmith, credited in the film, "Hatter's Castle," as I. Goldsmith, has also been credited as producer for other films as, I. G. Goldsmith, Isadore G. Goldsmith and Isidore Goldschmidt.

title_tesim: Albert Schweitzer and two boys in "Albert Schweitzer"
caption_tesim: Jerome Hill and Erica Anderson present "Albert Schweitzer" in Eastmancolor. Narrated by Fredric March and Burgess Meredith.

Text from back of photograph: "Albert Schweitzer" - Dr. Albert Schweitzer on the edge of the Ogowe River, where his jungle hospital is situated. A scene from "Albert Schweitzer", the definitive film biography of the Novel Prize winner who has achieved world-renown as a philosopher, theologian, missionary, physician, organist and, above all, as a man who has devoted his life to serving his fellow men. The Eastmancolor picture, produced and directed by Jerome Hill and photographed by Erica Anderson, took five years to make. It was filmed in Dr. Schweitzer's childhood village, Gunsbach, France and in the hospital-village in Lambarene, French Equatorial Africa. Narration by Fredric March and Burgess Meredith. Original score by Alec Wilder.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Many Hollywood headliners literally danced their way to fame. Joan Crawford, Ginger Rogers, Bob Hope, and George Murphy all began as dancers though today they're famed as dramatic actresses or comedy kings. No exception is comedian Billy De Wolfe, currently before the Paramount cameras in "Dear Ruth." Billy headlined at Europe's variety halls with an eccentric dance routine for many years before hitting the Hollywood sound stages.Once a hoofer, always a hoofer, says De Wolfe. Between scense of "Dear Ruth" he shows pert Virginia Welles how to tear off a schottishe or two.

title_tesim: Charlotte Paul and Marianna in Guerrilla Girl

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Excerpted from the AFI listing:"Guerrilla Girl" marked Greek producer-director John Christian's first American feature release. According to a Los Angeles Times news item, the actress billed as Marianna was working as Christian's production secretary before being cast in the film. A modern source lists the working title as "Dawn Comes Late."

Greek film producer/director, Giannis Hristodoulou, was credited in the film, "Guerrilla Girl" as, John Christian. He has also been credited in other films as, Jan Christian and Ioannis Hristodoulou, as well as by his birth name, Giannis Hristodoulou.
AFI lists a release date and copyright date for the film, "Guerrilla Girl,' as January 23, 1953.

title_tesim: Will Rogers as the police chief in The Golden Age of Comedy
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1924 silent film, "Big Moments from Little Pictures," starring Will Rogers.

In this photograph, a scene from the 1924 silent film, "Big Moments from Little Pictures," and highlighted in the 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," actor Will Rogers portrays 'Keystone Cop' actor, Ford Sterling, as the Police Chief.

Yet over the past year it has notably called attention to itself with films of unusual artistry that have received critical acclaim wherever shown, and won numerous festival awards. title_tesim: Candid photograph of Robert Paige and Andy Devine at the Houston, Texas premiere of The Green Promise
caption_tesim: Stamped and handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph:Theater Arts LibraryMPS-200
Handwritten in pencil text from back of photograph: MPS-200

Pasted-on paper with typed text from back of photograph: Robert Paige and Andy Devine alight at Glenn McCarthy's Shamrock Hotel in Houston for the dual premiere of the $21,000,000 hostelry and the Houston oil man's R-K-O movie, "The Green Promise".
Handwritten in purple ink text from back of photograph: 1
Stamped in blue ink text from back of photograph: East-WestPhotographic Agency
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: AFI lists the film, "The Green Promise," as having production dates of August 23 through early October of 1948. AFI also lists the film's release dates as: March 22, 1949; World premiere in Chicago, IL: December 2, 1948; Houston, Texas opening: March 18, 1949. By referring to the typed caption from the back of this photograph, the image is dated as March 18, 1949, the day of the film's Houston premiere.Also from the AFI listing:"The Green Promise" had its world premiere on 2 Dec 1948, during the annual 4-H Club Congress in Chicago, IL. According to Hollywood Reporter, McCarthy held a premiere in his hometown of Houston, Texas, in conjunction with the opening of his Shamrock Hotel, which was an $18,000,000 venture. More than forty "movie personalities" rode McCarthy's "Shamrock Special" train from Hollywood to Houston for the opening, which included a five-mile "torchlight parade of the stars," according to Hollywood Reporter. The top ticket price at the Houston opening was $4.65.

title_tesim: Robert Montgomery and Audrey Totter in a scene from The Saxon Charm
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal-International, Universal City, Calif."The Saxon Charm" -- A pictorial SynopsisBased on the novel by Frederic Wakeman (author of "The Hucksters"). "The Saxon Charm" is the story of Matt Saxon, theatrical producer, a genius at producing enemies. Written as a screenplay by Claude Binyon, who also directed, the movie stars Robert Montgomery in the meatiest role of his recent career. The film was produced by Joseph Sistrom for Universal-International. The story in pictures:9. Also while Saxon is in Hollywood Alma receives a phone call from her agent, cancelling a contract which would have given her a chance in films - because of rumors Saxon spread about her in Hollywood. When he returns to N.Y., Alma is waiting for him to tell him what she thinks of him. As Alma walks out of his life, Saxon threatens to poison himself.

title_tesim: Eve Arden, Olga San Juan, and Ava Gardner in One Touch Of Venus
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:From: Universal-InternationalUniversal City, Calif."ONE TOUCH OF VENUS" - - - A Pictorial Synopsis"One Touch of Venus" does more than successfully bring a hit musical show to the screen; it gives Ava Gardner what might well be called her showiest role in films. She plays Venus - and how! It also gives Robert Walker the top light comedy role of his career. Dick Haymes is their co-star, with Olga San Juan and Eve Arden featured. The film is produced by Lester Cowan for Universal-International release, with the show's original music by Kurt Weill and lyrics by Ogden Nash and Ann Ronnell. Wm. A. Seiter Directed. The story in pictures:7. When Gloria does meet Venus, the latter is under the wing of Mollie (EVE ARDEN), sharp-tongued secretary to Mr. Savory, who has ordered the new mannequin given "the works" to beautify her. Gloria and Mollie are, of course, amazed at how little they can do to improve upon Venus.7. title_tesim: Vera-Ellen, Richard Todd, and Cornel Wilde in London after filming Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: During the filming of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios, Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen had no chance of looking round London. She was either rehearsing or before the cameras every day of the production. So when at last, after five months hard work, her part in the film was completed Vera-Ellen decided to see the famous landmarks of England's capital for herself. Her guide and companion for the day was Britain's top male star, Richard (Hasty Heart) Todd, who was himself sightseeing in Hollywood only a few months earlier. Together, the two made a lightning tour of the city, visiting such well-known spots as Piccadilly Circus, Trafalgar Square, Fleet Street, Green Park and the Houses of Parliament. Well, it's a small world!, said Vera-Ellen to Richard Todd when they happened to run into famous Hollywood star Cornel Wilde as they were strolling down Piccadilly. Cornel Wilde was visiting Britain to negotiate a new film deal.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and unidentified dancers in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: A scene from the lively, haunting waltz number: One, Two, Three, featuring Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen and members of the Corpa de Ballet.In the film Happy-Go-Lovely, the One, Two, Three number takes place on the stage of the Mercury Theatre, Edinburgh, where Vera-Ellen, as Janet Jones, has taken over the part of leading lady.The music of One, Two, Three was written by Mischa Spoliansky and the choreography was arranged by Pauline Grant. Scenic designs, which are executed in delicate pastel shades to suit the mood of the dance, are provided by Art Director John Howell. The lilting, vivacious melody of the One, Two, Three waltz provides an excellent opportunity for Vera-Ellen to show her versatility as both dancer and singer. Happy-Go-Lovely is an Associated British-Marcel Hellman production in Technicolor starring David Niven, Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero. Bruce Humberstone directs this big-scale musical set against the background of the Edinburgh Festival.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and Richard Todd in London after filming Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: During the filming of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios, Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen had no chance of looking round London. So when at last, after five months hard work, her part in the film was completed Vera-Ellen decided to see the famous landmarks of England's capital for herself. Her guide and companion for the day was Britain's top male star, Richard (Hasty Heart) Todd, who was himself sightseeing in Hollywood only a few months earlier. Together, the two made a lightning tour of the city, visiting such well-known spots as Piccadilly Circus, Trafalgar Square, Fleet Street, Green Park and the Houses of Parliament. Walking through one of the many ancient alleyways off Fleet Street, in the city of London, are Richard Todd and Vera-Ellen. The sign above their heads gives the date of the building as 1700.
